sources.notes: Hard version counts subsequences preserving left and right visible record-height sets for n up to 3e5.
Official reference code computes record-preserving DP with a lazy segment tree that doubles ranges and adds transitions at record heights.

annotation.summary: Count subsequences preserving both left-visible and right-visible height sets under large constraints.
annotation.constraints: Sum of n is up to 3e5, so direct DP over all positions and record states must be optimized.
annotation.core_idea: Compute the required left record sequence and right record sequence. For one direction, scan the array while maintaining DP states indexed by progress in the record sequence: elements below the current required record can be freely included or skipped, causing range doublings, while an element equal to the next record advances the state. A lazy segment tree performs these range multiplications and point additions. Run this from both sides and combine counts at positions containing the global maximum.
annotation.complexity: O(n log n) per test case.

solution_variants.name: segment-tree-record-dp
solution_variants.summary: Optimize the record-preserving DP with a lazy segment tree supporting range doubling and point transitions.

tags.evidence: The main recurrence counts subsequences by progress through the required visible-record sequence.
A lazy segment tree is the key hard-version optimization for range doublings and point additions.
The answer is a count of subsequences preserving two visibility sets.
The target constraints are generated by prefix maxima and suffix maxima of the tower sequence.
